message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="heydon-pickering" color="blue" summary="Option A confirmed, nav link order, :has() edge case for clarify form">
Lea, this is a great response. You've identified the one real trade-off in my proposal (nav crowding) and already landed on the right answer.

## Nav Layout -- Option A, With One Refinement

I agree Option A is the right starting point. Flexbox wrapping is the correct CSS approach, and two rows of nav on mobile is perfectly fine. Screen readers don't know or care how many visual rows the nav occupies -- they just hear a list of links.

Option B (the "More" dropdown) is a usability and a11y tax. Disclosure widgets need `aria-expanded`, keyboard support, and they hide content behind an interaction. Every link hidden behind "More" is a link the user might not discover. Steve Krug would have a field day with that one.

Option C (bottom tab bar on mobile) is interesting but premature. It's a visual optimization we can add later if the wrapping looks bad. The HTML stays the same either way, so there's no architectural cost to deferring it. And from an a11y perspective, as you noted, screen readers don't care about visual position -- as long as the source order is logical.

One refinement for Option A: **consider the link order carefully.** The most-used GTD views should come first in source order:

1. Inbox (the entry point -- always check this first)
2. Next Actions (the "do" list -- what you work from)
3. Projects
4. Waiting For
5. Someday/Maybe
6. Review
7. Sign out (always last -- it's a destructive action, not a view)

This order matches the GTD workflow priority and means keyboard users reach the most important views first. On mobile, if the nav wraps to two rows, the top row has the primary views and the second row has the less-frequent ones. That's actually a nice natural hierarchy without any clever CSS.

## The `:has()` Pattern for Clarify -- One A11y Detail

Your CSS example is exactly right. One thing to handle: when fields are hidden with `display: none`, they're removed from the accessibility tree AND their values are not submitted with the form. That's actually perfect for us -- if the "project fields" are hidden because the user selected "single action," those fields shouldn't submit and shouldn't be announced. No cleanup needed.

But there's an edge case: if a user selects "project," fills in the project name, then changes their mind and selects "single action," the project fields disappear visually. If they submit, the project name field value is correctly excluded from the form submission (because `display: none` elements don't submit). Good.

However, if they switch BACK to "project," the previously entered project name should still be in the input. And it will be -- `display: none` hides the element but doesn't clear its value. So `:has()` + `display: none` gives us the right behavior for free. No JavaScript state management needed.

The `:has()` browser support point is well-taken. Our browser targets (last 2 versions of evergreen) all support it. The fallback of "all fields visible" is functional -- just less elegant. We could add a note in the form: "Fill in the fields that apply to your choice" as a fallback, but honestly I doubt we need it.

## Summary

We're aligned. Option A for nav, `:has()` for progressive disclosure, visible counts, simple context tags. All simplifications. You're right that simpler CSS usually means we're heading the right direction -- and from my side, simpler HTML almost always means better accessibility. When both are getting simpler simultaneously, that's the sweet spot.

Good call on updating Steve Schoger about the layout shift. The single-column, enhanced header nav is a significant change from a sidebar model and he'll need to adjust his visual hierarchy thinking accordingly.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Heydon confirms full alignment. His nav link ordering suggestion is a good catch -- it affects both keyboard navigation and natural visual wrapping. Let me acknowledge and relay the key detail.

input.content:
Heydon, all great points. Two things I want to highlight:

**Nav link order**: Your proposed order (Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Review, Sign out) is exactly right. It follows GTD workflow priority AND means keyboard users reach the most important views first. The natural wrapping on mobile creates a visual hierarchy for free -- primary views on the top row, less-frequent views on the second row. No clever CSS needed, just correct source order. I'll make sure this ordering is documented for whoever implements the nav.

**`:has()` + `display: none` behavior**: Thank you for confirming the edge case analysis. The fact that `display: none` excludes values from form submission but preserves them in the DOM is exactly the behavior we want. Users can toggle between choices without losing their input, and the server only receives the fields relevant to the current selection. This is one of those cases where the platform does the right thing by default -- no JavaScript state management needed.

Steve Schoger is on board with header nav (he sent me a detailed revised design). Carson confirmed `hx-boost` on `<body>`. The team is fully aligned. Simpler HTML, simpler CSS, better accessibility -- the sweet spot, as you said.

Good collaboration on this. Your pushback on the sidebar prevented us from building the wrong thing.
input.summary: Confirmed: nav link order and :has() behavior -- fully aligned

routing.summary: Confirmed: nav link order and :has() behavior -- fully aligned

content.text: All conversations from my area of expertise are resolved with full alignment. Standing by for the next phase -- implementation task assignments. Every major frontend/CSS decision has been discussed, debated where needed, and settled with team consensus.
